title: How ”safe” is the Romanian SAFE?
content:
Romania has a minority government without parliamentary backing and it could fall next week, when a vote on a motion of no confidence submitted by the Social Democrats and the far-right is scheduled.

In this complicated context, dark clouds have gathered over the SAFE programme, even though some projects were approved on Tuesday, April 28, in the defence committees of the Chamber of Deputies and the Senate. 

The leader of the far-right AUR party, George Simion, has asked Prime Minister Ilie Bolojan not to sign any major contracts during this period, including in the defence sector, and has warned public officials that they will be held accountable if they sign such contracts.

The motion of no confidence has been signed by 253 MPs, and if all of them vote for it, the government will be dismissed. Although the AUR leader wants to come to power, President Nicușor Dan said on Tuesday, April 28, that none of the pro-European parties wants to form a government with anti-Western forces.

He welcomed the pro-European direction and the responsibility of those who appr
snippet: Romania has a minority government without parliamentary backing and it could fall next week, when a vote on a motion of no confidence submitted by the Social Democrats and the far-right is scheduled.
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: The price of war – how the conflicts in Ukraine and the Middle East are driving up the costs
content:
The global and European economies have entered a state of permanent crisis, in which geopolitical turmoil has marked the end of the era of cheap energy and secure supply chains.

At the center of these events are the countries of Eastern Europe. The countries in this region, including Bulgaria, are feeling the direct weight of two overlapping global conflicts – the ongoing war in Ukraine and the conflict in the Middle East that erupted in early 2026 between the U.S. and Israel against Iran. The result for the end consumer is clear: a surge in prices for a wide range of goods – from the gas pump to the bread on the table.

The spiralling inflation in the EU began as early as February 2022 with Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, which forced Europe to abruptly stop purchasing Russian energy. For Bulgaria, which before the war imported over 90% of its natural gas and about 60% of its oil from Russia, this transition was difficult, but Russia’s share of pipeline gas supplies to the EU has already fallen sharply from about 40% in 2021 to approximately 6% by 2025.

“Proximity cost”

Although markets

title: Who’s paying up? Defence spending on the Eastern Flank [PODCAST]
content:
Tune in to the new episode of the EastFlank Podcast. This time, journalists from newsrooms across countries of NATO’s and the EU’s eastern flank discuss defence spending.


Donald Trump once again puts the issue of defence financing on a knife’s edge, while the European Union steps up its efforts to increase strategic autonomy in this area. We look at how countries on the eastern flank are navigating this complex and challenging reality.

Find out which countries spend the most on defence — and which approach these expenditures with greater scepticism. As always, the discussion is based on the latest EastFlank Report.

The post Who’s paying up? Defence spending on the Eastern Flank [PODCAST] appeared first on Eastflank.
snippet: Tune in to the new episode of the EastFlank Podcast. This time, journalists from newsrooms across countries of NATO’s and the EU’s eastern flank discuss defence spending. Donald Trump once again puts
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Defence spending on the Eastern Flank [INFOGRAPHIC]
content:
The data indicate a structural shift across the entire eastern flank of NATO. All analysed countries have reached or exceeded the 2% of GDP threshold for defence spending and have submitted applications for funding under the SAFE mechanism.

This demonstrates a growing conviction that defence has become one of the key pillars of state policy. However, this process is not uniform. The Baltic states, Finland, and Poland perceive the threat from Russia as direct and existential, which translates into a rapid increase in defence expenditure. By contrast, Czech Republic and Slovakia approach the issue more cautiously, limiting the pace of change for fiscal reasons.

You can read more about defence policy models on the EU/NATO eastern flank in the EastFlank Report.

The post Defence spending on the Eastern Flank [INFOGRAPHIC] appeared first on Eastflank.

title: Who’s paying up? Defence spending on the Eastern Flank
content:
Donald Trump has once again brought defence spending to the forefront of transatlantic tensions. The pressure he applied during his first presidency (2017–2021) for NATO allies to meet — and exceed — the 2% of GDP benchmark has resurfaced, driven by mounting uncertainty over the long-term reliability of U.S. security commitments.

The debate has taken on new urgency following the NATO summit in The Hague in June 2025, where allies agreed on a significantly more ambitious target: raising defence-related spending to 5% of GDP by 2035. Of this, 3.5% is allocated to core military capabilities, with a further 1.5% dedicated to broader security and resilience.

This higher benchmark reflects a growing recognition that the security environment has fundamentally worsened, requiring a substantial expansion in both the scale and scope of investment. 

Russia’s war against Ukraine, along with wider geopolitical instability, has exposed critical weaknesses in Europe’s security architecture. At the same time, Donald Trump’s return to the White House has brought renewed pressure on allies to increase defence spending, alongside uncert
snippet: Donald Trump has once again brought defence spending to the forefront of transatlantic tensions. The pressure he applied during his first presidency (2017–2021) for NATO allies to meet — and exceed —
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: False claim about EU mandating “cow masks” spread by senior Slovak official, based on pro-Russian source
content:
The European Commission confirmed to euBrief that the claim is false. The account Lukáš Machala cited has long been spreading content with an anti-European and pro-Kremlin tone.

On 22 April, Lukáš Machala, highly influential secretary general of the Ministry of Culture, wrote on Telegram that EU Commission President Ursula von der Leyen wants all European cows to wear masks. 

“[The masks] are produced by the American company Cargill, and the technology is meant to help reduce greenhouse gas emissions and contribute to the fight against global warming. So I’m not sure which image shows the bigger [cow],” he added in a post, which included a collage featuring an altered image of an animal next to Von der Leyen.

Source: Telegram of Lukáš Machala

Journalist Zuzana Kovačič Hanzelová from the Slovak outlet SME was the first to draw attention to the post on Instagram. 

Lukáš Macha

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Dispute Over Taiwan Visit Exposes Divisions in Czech Foreign Policy
content:
A political dispute among Czechia’s top constitutional officials has spilled into foreign policy, this time over relations with Taiwan.

Prime Minister Andrej Babiš refused to provide opposition Czech Senate Speaker Miloš Vystrčil with a government aircraft and described the trip as a “multi-million crown excursion,” a claim rejected by business leaders who were supposed to accompany Vystrčil.

Babiš’s decision to deny Vystrčil access to the government plane effectively downgraded the planned visit, which the prime minister described as unnecessary and costly. He defended the move by invoking the need for “pragmatic foreign policy,” alluding to the Czech Republic’s sensitive relationship with China. 

Beijing does not recognise Taiwan’s independence and expects countries, including Czechia, to adhere to the “One China” policy – under which Taiwan is considered part of China. Official visits to Taiwan are therefore a source of diplomatic friction.

Vystrčil, however, has repeatedly defied this stance in the past, drawing protests from the Chinese embassy in Prag

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: How Turkey became a problem for the EU
content:
Turkey remains, at least formally, a candidate for European Union membership. In practice, however, accession talks have long been frozen, and Brussels is increasingly uncertain how to frame its relationship with Ankara.

The long-postponed issue resurfaced during last week’s summit in Nicosia. Cypriot President Nikos Christodoulides said in June last year that he intended to invite Turkey (or Türkiye, as Ankara prefers) to the summit held under the Cypriot presidency of the Council of the EU — a position he reiterated in an interview with the Financial Times in November.

In the end, however, no invitation was extended, although several regional leaders are expected to attend. Asked about the matter, Cyprus’s Deputy Minister for European Affairs, Marilena Raouna, said at a press conference that the initial plan had been to invite

creator: Aleksandra Krzysztoszek

title: The Green Deal at a Crossroads: Can It Withstand the EU’s Eastern Flank?
content:
While leaders in Brussels speak of accelerating the green transition, scepticism still dominates in parts of the EU’s eastern flank, where political hesitation risks leaving the region behind at a moment when change is no longer a choice but a necessity.

Europe now faces a decision its leaders have postponed for decades. Christophe Jost, energy policy coordinator at CAN Europe, leaves little doubt about the stakes: “We are dealing with yet another crisis driven by fossil fuels — not the first in history. And we now face a choice: do we continue on the current path and look for ways to lower fossil fuel prices, or do we take a different approach and recognise that fossil fuels are what got us here, and that we need to move away from them.”

The conclusions of the Brussels summit, in which European leaders acknowledged the need to speed up the transition as the only way to reduce exposure to volatile energy prices, provide a clear direction. Yet there remains a significant gap between EU-level declarations and the energy realities of Central and Eastern Europe — one that cannot be bridged by summit communiqués alone.</
snippet: While leaders in Brussels speak of accelerating the green transition, scepticism still dominates in parts of the EU’s eastern flank, where political hesitation risks leaving the region behind at a mom
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Why MEP Diana Șoșoacă Isn’t Behind the Strait of Hormuz Reopening
content:
Romanian MEP Diana Șoșoacă claims that the discussions held on Friday, April 17, at the Inter-Parliamentary Union Assembly in Istanbul, in which she “actively” participated, convinced Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

In reality, the strait was reopened for a very short time, but as a result of U.S.-Iran negotiations mediated by Pakistan. The extremist MEP is known for her controversial statements, but during her participation in the Inter-Parliamentary Assembly in Istanbul, Diana Șoșoacă outdid herself.

“Following discussions at the Inter-Parliamentary Assembly in Istanbul, Turkey, in which I actively participated, the Strait of Hormuz was opened,” Șoșoacă wrote on social media, claiming she was Romania’s sole representative at the meeting in Turkey.

Shortly after her statement, Iran closed the strait again due to the U.S. blockade of Iranian ports. During the 152nd session of the Inter-Parliamentary Union Assembly in Istanbul, the conflict in the Middle East was also addressed – according to the official agenda – with an emphasis on the role of parliaments in establishing robust post-conflict manage
snippet: Romanian MEP Diana Șoșoacă claims that the discussions held on Friday, April 17, at the Inter-Parliamentary Union Assembly in Istanbul, in which she “actively” participated, convinced Iran to reopen t
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Romania is (once again) entering a political crisis
content:
“Born” on June 23, 2025, and officially “dead” on April 23, 2026, following the resignations of the Social Democratic (S&D) ministers from the government, the governing coalition comprising the PNL (EPP), USR (Renew), UDMR (EPP), and national minorities was doomed from the start.

From the very start of this new political arrangement – formed after Nicușor Dan’s election victory and justified by the need for a so-called “cordon sanitaire” against the extremist parties in Bucharest – AUR, POT, and SOS – the PSD expressed dissatisfaction from the very first days with Liberal Prime Minister Ilie Bolojan due to disputes centered primarily on economic policies. Subsequently, the 2026 state budget was the straw that broke the camel’s back.

The relationship between the PSD and the PNL, already eroded since the days of the old coalition led by Nicolae Ciucă and Marcel Ciolacu (the electoral failures of both parties, with particularly dismal results for the Social Democrats) have now reached a climax, as the main governing party has defected.
snippet: “Born” on June 23, 2025, and officially “dead” on April 23, 2026, following the resignations of the Social Democratic (S&D) ministers from the government, the governing coalition comprising the PN
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Same-sex marriages: a political challenge for Poland
content:
The issue of recognising same-sex marriages concluded abroad has turned into a political minefield in Poland, with the government lacking a clear plan on how to handle it.

“The situation is quite absurd. Poland has no real choice but to recognise marriages concluded abroad, as it has done so far. The only difference is that in this case, they involve same-sex couples,” said Bart Staszewski in an interview with FocusEurope.pl.

The dispute over the transcription of same-sex marriages in Poland intensified following landmark rulings by European and domestic courts. A key turning point was a ruling by the Court of Justice of the European Union in November 2025, which obliged member states to recognise such unions in certain situations under EU law.

Based on that judgment, the Supreme Administrative Court (NSA) ruled on 20 March 2026 that Polish authorities must transcribe foreign same-sex marriages, finding previous refusals to be in breach of, among other things, freedom of movement and th
snippet: The issue of recognising same-sex marriages concluded abroad has turned into a political minefield in Poland, with the government lacking a clear plan on how to handle it. “The situation is quite absu
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: “You can’t buy time with money” – how will we spend 5% of GDP on defence?
content:
Larger ammunition stockpiles, new drone and counter-drone capabilities, rearming of the Mechanised Infantry Brigade, and much more – the Ministry of Defence (MoD) has no shortage of plans on how to spend 5% of Latvia’s gross domestic product (GDP) annually on strengthening the country’s armed forces, as provided for by a law recently adopted by the Saeima.

Does this law truly guarantee that the money will be there? Experts surveyed by Delfi admitted that it does not. Rather, it is a signal to international partners that Latvia will fulfil what has been promised to NATO and the United States. At the same time, the army has previously been “starved” for a long period, so now it must be hoped that ambitious security plans will be enabled not only by money, but also by time.

The State Defence Financing Law consists of two articles. The first defines the aim of the law – “to purposefully increase national defence capabilities through financial means”. The second specifies the schedule for increasing defence funding, expressed as a percentage of GDP.

The current law was adopted in the summer of 2014 after Russia occupied Ukrai
snippet: Larger ammunition stockpiles, new drone and counter-drone capabilities, rearming of the Mechanised Infantry Brigade, and much more – the Ministry of Defence (MoD) has no shortage of plans on how to sp
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: What lies behind Poland’s constitutional crisis
content:
Poland’s parliament has appointed six judges to the Constitutional Tribunal (TK), but the president has sworn in only two of them. In response, parliament took matters into its own hands, and the remaining four were sworn in without the president’s participation. This is the latest chapter in Poland’s constitutional crisis.

It is hard to imagine some of the country’s most distinguished legal experts — appointed by a democratically elected parliament to serve on the highest court — spending their working days sitting in corridor armchairs or the court library instead of delivering rulings in their robes. They have no offices, no cases to hear, and the court’s president does not recognise their status.

Yet this surreal image is not the plot of a political fiction film, but the everyday reality of Poland’s Constitutional Tribunal in 2026.

For an outside observer, the situation may seem incomprehensible. To understand how this deadlock came about, one must first grasp what the Constitutional Tribunal is in Poland — and why control over it has become a political holy grail.

The Tribunal is not an ordinary court. Its m

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Complicated coalition talks and a scandal in the background. How did Slovenia conduct its parliamentary elections?
content:
Last month, Slovenian citizens went to the polls to elect the 90 members of the lower house of parliament.

Although no political party won a clear majority, Prime Minister Robert Golob’s liberals managed to edge slightly ahead of right-wing populist leader Janez Janša.

The elections were closely watched beyond Slovenia as well. The vote was seen as a kind of mood test among voters in the bloc, as right-wing forces increasingly gain ground in the EU’s political landscape. It was believed that Janša’s return to government would have influenced the country’s position in broader EU debates.

Who actually won?

In the vote on the 22nd of March, the main contest was between incumbent Prime Minister Robert Golob and his liberal party “Freedom Movement” (GS), and Slovenia’s three-time former prime minister Janez Janša with the right-wing Slovenian Democratic Party (SDS). Opinion polls had not predicted a clear majority for any political force in Slovenia’s 90-seat parliament.

“Freedom Movement” won 28.63% of the vote and 29 seats in pa

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Orban is gone, Radev is here. Has Moscow gained a new friend in the EU?
content:
In April, the elections in Hungary were undoubtedly in the spotlight. Ultimately, they brought an end to the 16-year rule of pro-Kremlin Prime Minister Viktor Orbán and raised hopes for a new beginning in Budapest’s relations with the European Union (EU).

But will this Kremlin ally’s place remain vacant? There are several prominent politicians in Europe whose rhetoric at least partially aligns with Kremlin ideas, and one of them won parliamentary elections in Bulgaria just a week after Victor Orban’s defeat.

Hungary in the spotlight

Hungary’s parliamentary elections attracted enormous media and public attention, as pre-election polls suggested a looming defeat for Orban, who had been in power since 2010 and had become the closest ally of Russian dictator Vladimir Putin within the EU.

In addition to Budapest’s disputes with Brussels over the erosion of democratic norms—leading to the freezing of 18 billion euros in EU subsidies — Orban in recent years distinguished himself by openly accommodating the aggressor state, obstructing sanctions against Moscow and block
snippet: In April, the elections in Hungary were undoubtedly in the spotlight. Ultimately, they brought an end to the 16-year rule of pro-Kremlin Prime Minister Viktor Orbán and raised hopes for a new beginnin
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Can a house and land be taken away in Latvia for not paying taxes?
content:
“You have bought a house or a flat. You have land. But every year you have to pay a “TAX”. If you don’t pay = THEY WILL TAKE IT AWAY. So answer honestly: is it your property, or you just have the right to use it temporarily?” This provocative post was published in April on X by boxer and current Riga City Council member Mairis Briedis.

He was elected from the Stabilitātei! (“For Stability!”) party list, but now sits as an independent councillor.

Mairis Briedis has previously drawn public attention with controversial statements — particularly after Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, when he refused to take a clear stance, criticised the media, and later opposed the demolition of the so-called Victory Monument. Meanwhile, the party he once represented is now close to falling apart. In the past, Stabilitātei! had promised voters to abolish real estate tax (RET) for a primary residence.

Briedis told Delfi that by “tax” he meant property tax on land and buildings. If it is not paid for a year or two, the property is “taken away”, he claims. Enforcement proceedings begin after about a yea

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Fact check: was free coffee handed out in Riga during Soviet times?
content:
The social media page “Unusual Riga” has gained considerable popularity, regularly posting various entries that romanticise and beautify the Soviet period. Among them is one publication featuring an AI-generated image in which well-dressed women from Riga, smiling children, and soldiers in Dome Square are enjoying “free coffee” from plastic cups.

The image is fake because real coffee was difficult to obtain during the Soviet times, let alone for free. Meanwhile, the romanticisation of the Soviet era is a tool of propaganda and hybrid warfare, continuing even today to conceal the severe economic, social, and political consequences in the societies of every country occupied by Russia.

The page, under its Russian-language name, can be found both on Facebook and, under its English-language name, on Instagram. On both platforms, various seemingly harmless scenes and drawings from recent history are from time to time mixed with ideologically charged content.

The misleading image of “free coffee” is not the only one that encourages nostalgia for Soviet times. Looking through the “Unusual Riga” accounts on Instagram and Facebook,
snippet: The social media page “Unusual Riga” has gained considerable popularity, regularly posting various entries that romanticise and beautify the Soviet period. Among them is one publication featuring an A
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Can Péter Magyar deliver? Hungary’s EU funding battle reaches crunch point in Brussels
content:
Hungary’s incoming government faces an urgent and complex challenge: securing billions in frozen EU funds before critical deadlines expire. Success would boost both the economy and Péter Magyar’s political standing – failure could prove costly on both fronts.

Even if only by a narrow margin, Péter Magyar and his team may still be able to meet the deadline – and they have little choice if they do not want to forfeit vast amounts of EU funding.

One of Péter Magyar’s key campaign promises was to recover EU funds due to Hungary that have been frozen in several stages since 2020. The absence of these resources has contributed to the country’s economic difficulties, and their recovery could help restart growth and stabilise the budget.

Not least, it would also represent a major political success for Magyar, strengthening his legitimacy and delivering tangible results to voters quickly.

On Wednesday, Magyar will meet Ursula von der Leyen in Brussels to discuss unlocking the funds. This will be the first high-level in-person meeting between the two sides since the 12 Ap
snippet: Hungary’s incoming government faces an urgent and complex challenge: securing billions in frozen EU funds before critical deadlines expire. Success would boost both the economy and Péter Magyar’s poli
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Political advertising exploits loopholes on digital platforms
content:
Meta and Google banned paid political advertising in the EU at the end of 2025. The spring elections in Denmark and Hungary showed that the ban is being circumvented in various ways. According to experts, the new transparency rules on political advertising may do more harm than good.

The parliamentary elections in Denmark and Slovenia in March and the elections in Hungary in April were the first to test the effectiveness of the EU’s new political advertising regulations. The regulations, which came into force last October, oblige digital platforms, under threat of fines, to clearly disclose the source of funding for political advertisements. Targeted political advertisements now also require the consent of the viewers. As a result of the regulation, Meta and Google stopped publishing political adverts within the EU.

However, Danish politician Jacob Mark managed to circumvent the ban in January. Mark, who had previously represented the Green Left Party (Socialistisk Folkepar
